elements.sent-2411-001: Additionally, for high dimensional \added{regression problems, variable selection methods are also used to find a subset of predictor variables \added{that maximises the predictive performance of the \added{underlying model for better estimation of the causal effect.}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper, we \added{propose a different approach.}
elements.sent-2411-003: We focus on the variable selection aspects of high dimensional causal estimation problem.
elements.sent-2411-004: We suggest a cautious Bayesian group LASSO (least absolute shrinkage and selection operator) framework for variable selection using prior sensitivity analysis.
elements.sent-2411-005: We argue that in some cases, abstaining from selecting (or, rejecting) a predictor is beneficial and we should gather more information to obtain a more decisive result.
elements.sent-2411-006: We also show that for problems with very limited information, expert elicited variable selection can give us a more stable causal effect estimation as it avoids overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-007: Lastly, we carry a comparative study with synthetic dataset and show the applicability of our method in real-life situations.
elements.sent-2411-008: Causal inference using observational data is important in many fields, including epidemiology, social science, economics, and many more.
elements.sent-2411-009: Causal inference concerns estimating the causal effect of predictor variables on an outcome variable, as well as identifying which predictors are causally linked with the outcome.
elements.sent-2411-010: Ideally, randomised trials are the most efficient way to perform this task.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, this is not always practical due to, for instance, ethical concerns, design cost, population size, to name a few.
elements.sent-2411-012: This leaves us with observational studies where data is collected though surveys or record keeping.
elements.sent-2411-013: Still, it is highly desirable to try to adjust for confounding in our statistical models to the best of our ability.
elements.sent-2411-014: This is termed (perhaps somewhat unfortunately) causal inference in the literature \citep{rubin1978,rosenbaum83,Robins1986ANA,winship99,stuart10,Zigler2014,wang2015,koch2018,Hahn2018,koch2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: This is also the approach that we will follow here, under the disclaimer that whether actual causality can be inferred remains a subject of interpretation and conjecture specific to the situation being studied.
elements.sent-2411-016: In this regard, we also refer to \citep{imbiens_2022 where a detailed discussion on different interpretations of `causality' in statistics and econometrics can be found.}
elements.sent-2411-017: A confounder is any variable which is causally linked with both a predictor and the outcome, giving the false impression that that predictor causes the outcome (see \cref{fig:confounding ).}
elements.sent-2411-018: Confounding happens commonly in observational treatment studies because many predictors are often causally linked with the treatment decision (which is also a predictor), whilst simultaneously affecting the outcome of the treatment.
elements.sent-2411-019: Any such predictors act as a confounders between treatment decision (as one of the predictor variables) and treatment outcome.
elements.sent-2411-020: In such cases, we must be extra cautious as we risk unwanted bias in the causal effect estimator \citep{rosenbaum83 , if we ignore such correlations.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Several authors have tackled the presence of confounder variables.
elements.sent-2411-022: Subsequently, several other methods have been proposed based on propensity score matching; see \citep{winship99,stuart10 for a brief review.}
elements.sent-2411-023: One of the earlier Bayesian approaches to causal inference can be found in \citep{rubin1978 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: More recently, with the rise of high dimensional data, Bayesian methodologies have grown in popularity.
elements.sent-2411-025: Several other methods were proposed to tackle confounders, see for instance \citep{Zigler2014,Hahn2018 among others \added{for a Bayesian perspective and \citep{zhang_2018 for a survey of methods for addressing unmeasured confounding.}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: In this paper, we take inspiration from the approach of \citet{koch2020 , who proposed a bi-level spike and slab prior for causal effect estimation \added{in high dimensional problems (i.e.~when number of predictors is larger than the number of observations) .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: They considered a data-driven adaptive approach to propose their prior which reduces the variance of the causal estimate.
elements.sent-2411-028: Our approach \added{however focuses on the other aspect of high dimensional causal inference problem, ie.~variable selection.}
elements.sent-2411-029: To achieve that we rely on \added{prior sensitivity analysis, where instead of using a single prior, we consider a set of priors \citep{BERGER1990303 .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: However, variable selection in causal estimation problem has not been investigated in robust Bayesian framework.
elements.sent-2411-031: This motivates us to \added{investigate the role and applicability of prior sensitivity analysis in high dimensional \added{causal estimation problems.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: This is particularly beneficial, as in high dimensional problems, we have to rely on very limited \added{observations to perform our Bayesian analysis and \added{as a result variable selection with a single prior can be unreliable \citep{basu2023robust \added{in many cases, Moreover, in causal effect estimation, failing to correctly identify a relation between the treatment effect and predictor can lead to harmful side-effects.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Therefore, it is extremely important to adopt a cautious approach in selecting or rejecting a variable.
elements.sent-2411-034: To \added{achieve this cautious paradigm and to perform a prior sensitivity analysis, we rely on expert opinion to elicit a set of priors based on empirical evidence.}
elements.sent-2411-035: This allows us to construct the problem of predictor selection in a framework where abstention has a relatively positive gain i.e.~when the cost of further tests/data collection is \added{lower than that of incorrectly treating a subject.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Our framework considers a set of continuous spike and slab priors \citep{ishwaran2005 for predictor selection.}
elements.sent-2411-037: We thereby construct a Bayesian group LASSO \added{(least absolute shrinkage and selection operator) \citep{xu2015 type problem.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: To perform sensitivity analysis, we consider a set of beta priors on the covariate selection probability of the spike and slab priors.
elements.sent-2411-039: We use the posteriors of this covariate selection probability for identifying the active predictors.
elements.sent-2411-040: Finally, we consider a post-hoc coefficient adjustment method \citep{hahn2015 to recover sparse estimates associated with either the outcome or the treatment model.}
elements.sent-2411-041: The rest of the paper is organised as follows.
elements.sent-2411-042: In \cref{sec:causal we give a formal description of the causal estimation problem in the context of linear regression.}
elements.sent-2411-043: In \cref{sec:sim , we provide results of simulation studies under different scenarios and show the possible applications in real life problems.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Finally, we discuss our findings and conclude this paper in \cref{sec:conc .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Let an observational study on $n$ individuals give us treatment outcomes $Y\coloneqq(Y_1, \dots, Y_n)$ with corresponding treatment decisions $T\coloneqq(T_1, \dots, T_n)$.
elements.sent-2411-046: Here, we use an indicator to represent the treatment decision.
elements.sent-2411-047: That is, $T_i$ is $1$ if the $i$th patient was treated, and $0$ otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-048: Similarly, $Y_i$ is the treatment outcome of the $i$th patient, represented as some real-valued quantity.
elements.sent-2411-049: Regression methods are widely used in causal effect estimation.
elements.sent-2411-050: The main idea behind these regression methods is to remove the correlation between the treatment indicator and the error term \citep{winship99,HECKMAN1985 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: To do so, we rely on $p$ observed quantities, called predictors, denoted by $X\coloneqq$ $[X_1^{\top , \dots, X_n^{\top ]^{\top}$ where each $X_i\in\mathbb{R ^p$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Each $X_i$ is treated as a $p$-dimensional row vector, so $X$ is a $n\times p$ matrix.
elements.sent-2411-053: Now, let $\beta \coloneqq (\beta_1$, \dots, $\beta_p)^{\top}$ denote the vector of regression coefficients related to the predictors, and let $\beta_T$ denote a regression coefficient related to the treatment decision.
elements.sent-2411-054: Note that both $T_i$ and $X_i$ are predictors for $Y_i$ in the above model.
elements.sent-2411-055: However, when we talk about predictors in this paper, we usually mean just the components of $X_i$.
elements.sent-2411-056: To decide whether or not to treat a new individual with given predictors, we are mainly interested in the effect of the treatment on the outcome.
elements.sent-2411-057: More precisely, the causal effect of a new individual, indexed as $n+1$, whose outcome $Y_{n+1}$ is not yet observed, and with observed predictors $X_{n+1 =x_{n+1}$, is defined by: \begin{equation} \delta(x_{n+1}) \coloneqqE(Y_{n+1}\mid X_{n+1}=x_{n+1},T_{n+1} =1) \qquad - E(Y_{n+1}\mid X_{n+1}=x_{n+1},T_{n+1}=0)  \intertext{For our model, due to linearity of expectation, we have that}  \delta(x_{n+1}) =\beta_T +x_{n+1}\beta+E(\epsilon_{n+1}\mid X_{n+1}=x_{n+1},T_{n+1} =1) \qquad\,\,\, -x_{n+1}\beta-E(\epsilon_{n+1}\mid X_{n+1}=x_{n+1},T_{n+1} =0)  \intertext{and because $\epsilon_{n+1}$ is independent from $X_{n+1}$ and $T_{n+1}$,} =\beta_{T}.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-226: So we also explore the effect of prior elicitation in identifying the active variables in our model.
elements.sent-2411-227: As mentioned earlier, we consider $c$ is expected to be lying in the interval $[0.15,0.35]$ based on \cref{tab:corr .}
elements.sent-2411-228: However, we might want to choose a different value for $c$.
elements.sent-2411-229: To compare the effect of having different value for $c$, we use the case 2b and set $c\in[0.2,0.4]$.
elements.sent-2411-230: That is we set a higher threshold for the correlation so that $\overline{k}$ becomes smaller and hence the prior expectation of the inclusion probability.
elements.sent-2411-232: In the left hand side we elicit the expected number of active variables by setting the marginal correlation threshold $c\in[0.15,0.35]$ and the right we set $c\in[0.2,0.4]$.
elements.sent-2411-233: On the left hand side, we see our method tends to give higher number of indeterminate variables for fewer observations than the right hand side.
elements.sent-2411-234: As we increase the predictors the higher threshold of marginal correlation plays an important role and we see more cases of false negative variables on the right hand side.
elements.sent-2411-235: This also results to over estimation of the causal effect as many true active variables are shrinked towards zero.
elements.sent-2411-236: As a result the lower bound of the averaged causal effect is more than four on the right hand side.
elements.sent-2411-237: The analyses and simulation studies can be investigated using the code from \url{https://github.com/tathagatabasu/Causal-Inference .}
elements.sent-2411-238: Causal effect estimation is an important tool in statistical learning.
elements.sent-2411-239: Especially in risk-sensitive situations, such as medicine, it needs to be performed \added{with the utmost care as in many cases poor estimation can have severe adverse consequences.}
elements.sent-2411-240: In this paper, we tackle this issue by proposing a robust Bayesian analysis of the causal effect estimation problem for high dimensional data.
elements.sent-2411-241: Our framework is focused on the effect of prior elicitation on predictor selection as well as causal effect estimation.
elements.sent-2411-242: We consider a spike and slab type prior for predictor selection and discuss the possible sources of uncertainty that need to be tackled carefully.
elements.sent-2411-243: We were particularly focused on the uncertainty associated with prior selection probabilities for which we consider a set of beta priors to perform sensitivity analysis.
elements.sent-2411-244: We showed that the sensitivity analysis on the prior selection probability gives us a robust predictor selection scheme.
elements.sent-2411-245: In this way, we can abstain from selecting a predictor when the available data is not sufficient.
elements.sent-2411-246: We also propose a more relaxed utility based framework, where we associate a loss for abstaining which can be interpreted as the cost of further data collection.
elements.sent-2411-247: We illustrate our method with synthetic dataset and compare with other state of the art Bayesian methods.
elements.sent-2411-248: Moreover, we also notice correct elicitation of the inclusion probability plays a crucial role in identifying the active variables and therefore can be extremely useful in cases where we need to design a treatment guideline with multiple bio-markers.
elements.sent-2411-249: Currently, the paper proposes a robust Bayesian approach for causal effect estimation where we rely on sampling strategies to obtain the posterior bounds as well as performing variable selection.
elements.sent-2411-250: However, there is ample opportunity to improve computational aspects.
elements.sent-2411-251: However, loss functions could be used for a generalised decision theoretic framework as well.
elements.sent-2411-252: For instance, the selection problem itself could be formulated as a decision problem, potentially leading to different selection thresholds or even selection systems that are directly based on a loss function.
elements.sent-2411-253: Additionally, we could formulate the problem of whether or not to treat a subject as a decision support problem based on predictor selection.
elements.sent-2411-254: It has been shown that using bounded probability is particularly suitable for dealing with instances where data cannot be assumed missing at random \citep{decoomanzaffalon2004 .}
elements.sent-2411-255: Incorporating robustness against missing data could lead to an interesting extension of the model in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-256: We also notice with our simulation studies that our method tends to underestimate the causal effect when \added{only confounders are present in the model.}
elements.sent-2411-257: This suggests that we might want to use a correction formula for the causal effect.
elements.sent-2411-258: Moreover, in future, we would like to investigate different elicitation strategies for different prior parameters and their importance in causal effect estimation.
elements.sent-2411-259: In general, we noticed that our method is in good agreement with other methods with an added level of robustness.
elements.sent-2411-260: This shows that our method has good potential for real-life problems, and we intend to apply it on a real dataset in future work.
